texts.label: text
texts.orig: A flow  microcalorimeter and  vibrating  tube densimeter  were used  to obtain  apparent molal  heat  capacities and volumes  of aqueous NaHS and NazS  from 0.1 to 1.0 mol kg-'  and  of aqueous H2S  from 0.03 to 0.08 mol kg-'  at 10,25,  and 40°C. Standard state heat capacities and volumes for H2S  and HS-  were obtained by extrapolation to infinite dilution. Combining these results with 2S°C enthalpy data yields an expression for the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant for H2S  neutralization  at pressures near those of steam saturation, H2S + OH-= HS-+ H20,  log K,b = 19.84 + 930.8lT -2.800 In T, with an estimated uncertainty of  k0.47  at  300°C. The  heat  capacity  data  for  bulk  aqueous  NazS suggest  that  the  relative  concentration  of  SZ- at  these molalities is small.

The  heat  capacity  data  for  bulk  aqueous  NazS suggest  that  the  relative  concentration  of  SZ- at  these molalities is small.

Low temperature measurements have been  made  by  many  workers  using a  variety  of techniques (1) and the values for K, near 2 5 0 ~ are well  established. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Commercial  purity  H,S (Fisher)  was  bubbled  through  a saturated Ba(OH),  solution in a sealed glass system, which had been thoroughly purged with helium, then on into a sealed flask containing  water  or a  1 tn NaOH  solution  to  prepare  stock solutions of HIS and NaHS, respectively.  In the latter case, a sealed pH electrode allowed us to stop the gas flow when the pH fell to a value between 10 and 8, indicating that the composition was  stoichiometric to  within  0.01%  in  molality.  Solutions  of NaHS were also prepared from well defined  crystals,  synthesized  in  ethanol  by  reacting  H,S  with  sodium  ethoxide  using the method of Stephens and Cobble (1  1). The crystals were then washed thoroughly in diethyl ether under a nitrogen atmosphere and  stored  in  a  vacuum  desiccator  (0.01  Pa).  Well-formed crystals of analytical  reagent grade Na,S  (Mallinckrodt)  were recrystallized twice from filtered hot aqueous  solutions immediately  before  use.  To  minimize  oxidation,  the  solutions  were prepared by  quickly  removing large well-formed  crystals from the recrystallization flask, washing in deoxygenated water, then dissolving them in a second flask of water, all in a nitrogen glove bag. The final clear colourless solutions had a pH of about 13.5 and the approximate concentration of Na,S  in each was easily obtained by measuring the density. texts.orig: prepared by weight using a N, glove bag for all transfers. During the calorimetric  runs,  the solutions  of  NaHS and  Na,S  were contained in  closed  50 mL polythene  bottles,  which  had been filled under nitrogen, and were siphoned into the calorimeter or densimeter via a short length of 0.8 mm i.d. Teflon tubing which passed through a small hole in the bottle cap. Immediately after each  set  of  measurements,  aliquots  from  each  bottle  were quickly pipetted  into a flask containing the sulfide antioxidant buffer SAOB I1 (12) so that the sulfide concentration could be determined  by  titration  with  standardized  Pb(CIO,), solution using an Orion model 94-16 sulfide ion electrode to detect the end point.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The H,S solutions, prepared by bubbling in a closed system at 1  atm, were handled and standardized  in  the same way except that the solutions were contained in closed Pyrex flasks to avoid H,S  evaporation. Both  the H,S  solutions and the water reference solution were connected to the same cylinder of ultra high purity  nitrogen  so  that  manipulating  a  three-way  stopcock allowed either to be pressurized. A second three-way stopcock allowed  the  H,S  solution  or the  water to  be  forced  into  the calorimeter  or densimeter by  the overpressure  of  nitrogen.  A needle valve downstream of the instruments controlled the flow rate. Before and after each run, the solutions were standardized by diverting the tubing so  that the solution flowed directly under the  surface of a  weighed  aliquot  of  SAOB 11. The amount  of solution which passed into the SAOB I1 was measured by noting the  weight gain  and  the  amount  of  sulfide  determined by titration, as above.

texts.orig: crystals  were  more  negative  than  those prepared from H,S  and aqueous NaOH, by  about 5 J  K-I mol-' in C,+  and smaller by about 1  cm3  mol-I  in V+. Synthesized crystals which had been stored under a  pumping  vacuum  for  several  days  and  washed commercial crystals produced solutions  which yielded even more negative values. Thus, while the very similar values which were obtained from the two  preparation routes  confirm  that no  major impurities are present, we believe that the solutions prepared by bubbling H2S  through aqueous NaOH yield  superior  results.  The  crystalline  NaHS  is probably slightly  contaminated  with  sulfate  or thiosulfate, which are known to have very negative aqueous heat capacities and volumes (14) or possibly by polysulfides.

texts.orig: Heat capacities and volumes  of  simple electrolytes are known (29-31)  to increase with temperature to a maximum near 100"C,  then to decrease as the temperature is raised further. Above 200"C, this decrease  becomes  dramatic,  largely  because  of changes  in  the  electrostatic  properties  of  water along the steam saturation line (32,33). The simple electrostatic effects tend to cancel when reactions are written  symmetrically,  so that  the  charge  on each reactant species is equal to that on one of the product  species,  as  in  reaction  [6].  Hence,  for symmetric,  or "isocoulombic" (31), reactions, ACpRO and AVRO might be expected to pass through only a shallow maximum or minimum depending on the relative magnitude of the electrostatic effect for each  ion.  This  behavior  has  been  observed  for several systems (29, 31), and is well illustrated by the  very  accurate  data  recently  reported  for  the neutralization of phosphoric acid and carbonic acid (34) at elevated temperatures. Below 250"C, values for ACpRO(T) for both  systems  vary  by  less than 50 J  K-I  mol-I  from ACpR0(25"C).  Since the low temperature  heat  capacities  in  Table  4  decrease with temperature, ACpRO for reaction [6] probably passes through a minimum,  shown schematically by  curve A in Fig. 3.

Both sets of experimental  values  of  K,  (2,  3)  may  contain systematic  errors,  since  neither  apparatus  provided  a  mechanism for  measuring the  equilibrium pH.  Our  results  for  log  K,  below  15O"C deviate from those of  Tsonopoulos et al. by no more than 0.05  at  any  temperature  and  therefore  do  not support the more negative values reported by Ellis and Giggenbach (2). Consequently, at higher temperatures, the extrapolated data in Table 5  are quite possibly  more  accurate  than  the  direct  measurements described in ref. 2.

texts.orig: Conclusions
texts.text: Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The apparent  molal  volume  and  heat  capacity results reported here yield standard state values for HS-  and H2S  and permit the extrapolation of  low temperature  equilibrium  constants,  as  presented above. Equally important, the H,S  heat capacities and high temperature values of Kl calculated from CP+O(H2S) and CpO(HS-)  provide a sensitive test of the  results  reported  by  Clark  and  Glew  (17)  and Tsonopoulos et al.  (3),  respectively.  The  agreement between data obtained using such very different techniques increases  confidence  in  the accuracy  of all  these  results  and  also  of the  other constants ( 3 , 4 , 19) used in the calculations, so that self-consistent  thermodynamic  functions  for  hydrogen  sulfide and  the  hydrogen  sulfide  ion  can easily be  calculated  (see Appendix).  Finally,  the Na2S  heat capacities reinforce other evidence that, despite much uncertainty in its stability constant, the sulfide ion, S2-, is  at most  a minor species in solutions less alkaline than p O H z  0.5.
texts.text: The apparent  molal  volume  and  heat  capacity results reported here yield standard state values for HS-  and H2S  and permit the extrapolation of  low temperature  equilibrium  constants,  as  presented above. Equally important, the H,S  heat capacities and high temperature values of Kl calculated from CP+O(H2S) and CpO(HS-)  provide a sensitive test of the  results  reported  by  Clark  and  Glew  (17)  and Tsonopoulos et al.  (3),  respectively.  The  agreement between data obtained using such very different techniques increases  confidence  in  the accuracy  of all  these  results  and  also  of the  other constants ( 3 , 4 , 19) used in the calculations, so that self-consistent  thermodynamic  functions  for  hydrogen  sulfide and  the  hydrogen  sulfide  ion  can easily be  calculated  (see Appendix).  Finally,  the Na2S  heat capacities reinforce other evidence that, despite much uncertainty in its stability constant, the sulfide ion, S2-, is  at most  a minor species in solutions less alkaline than p O H z  0.5.

texts.orig: for AGfO(H2S,g).  Combining these with Clark and Glews' vapour pressure measurements (17) yields the values for H2S(aq),  except  for V0 and CpO which are from this work. Standard ionic heat capacities and  volumes  (CpO(H+) = 0,  VO(H+) = 0)  were calculated for HS- using Allred and Woolley's data (16) and our results. Combining the data for H2S(aq) with log KIb (7) and AHRO for reaction [6]  (3,4) yields AGfO(HSP),  AHfO(HS-) and, hence, SO(HS-).  As in refs.  19-22, the standard state gas reference pressure is  101.325  kPa.
texts.text: for AGfO(H2S,g).  Combining these with Clark and Glews' vapour pressure measurements (17) yields the values for H2S(aq),  except  for V0 and CpO which are from this work. Standard ionic heat capacities and  volumes  (CpO(H+) = 0,  VO(H+) = 0)  were calculated for HS- using Allred and Woolley's data (16) and our results. Combining the data for H2S(aq) with log KIb (7) and AHRO for reaction [6]  (3,4) yields AGfO(HSP),  AHfO(HS-) and, hence, SO(HS-).  As in refs.  19-22, the standard state gas reference pressure is  101.325  kPa.
